settings.title: What a Day in the Pipeworks Van Actually Looks Like
settings.excerpt: No two jobs run the same, and the tidy ones are rarely the ones that stick in the memory.
settings.body:
People picture plumbing as one big dramatic burst. Most days are quieter and stranger than that, and the variety is half the reason the job never gets dull.

A recent Tuesday for our engineer Dec started with a dripping radiator valve, moved to a child's toy wedged firmly in a toilet trap, and ended with an elderly customer whose only real problem was a tap washer worn paper thin. Three jobs, three streets, and not one of them what the morning had promised.

The job that mattered most

The toy job took the longest, an hour of careful work to free it without cracking the pan or making a worse mess. The tap took four minutes and a fifty-pence part, and got the warmest thank you of the day. The customer had been turning it off with a spanner for months and assumed the whole tap needed replacing.

  • A worn washer rarely means a new tap
  • A slow toilet can be a blockage, not a fault
  • Most callouts end with a small part, not a big bill

We carry common spares on every van precisely because of days like that. Washers, valves, seals, and the odd-sized fittings that catch people out are all on board. The small fix done on the spot beats a second visit every time, for you and for us.

If you are ever unsure whether something is worth a callout, ring and describe it. More often than not we can tell you over the phone whether it is a five-minute job or something that genuinely needs us out.
